summary: Jeff Atwood argues against isolating developers from end users, a practice he calls 'ivory tower development.' He contends that when developers work in isolation, shielded by business analysts, they inevitably create worse products because they assume users think like developers. The post advocates for exposing developers directly to users throughout the project lifecycle, noting that developers must witness firsthand how typical users struggle with software. Atwood extends this critique to internal enterprise software, suggesting that captive audiences lead to poor quality, and proposes that internal departments should operate like micro-ISVs selling to their users.
key_points: Isolated developers assume all users are developers, leading to products that fail real users
Business analysts often worsen the problem by shielding developers from direct user contact
Internal enterprise software tends to be poor quality because users have no choice but to use it
Developers must directly observe users struggling with software to overcome their 'Homo Logicus' blinders
Internal departments should operate like micro-ISVs, charging for their software to create natural market pressure for quality
money_quotes: In the absence of any other compelling evidence, developers assume everyone else is a developer.
The more isolated the developers, the worse the resulting end product.
Nothing removes a developer's Homo Logicus blinders faster than seeing a typical user struggle with basic computer applications.
Developers simply cannot comprehend that the average user doesn't even know what ALT+TAB does, much less how to use it. They have to see it to believe it.
I define internal projects as projects where users are forced to use your application whether they want to or not.
Sadly, lots of internal software sucks pretty badly. It's true. And it is sad.
I'd much rather work on projects with paying customers, or at least treat internal projects as if users were paying real money for your product.
key_insight: Software quality degrades in direct proportion to how isolated developers are from their users, and the only cure is forcing developers to witness real users struggling with their creations.
